questions.stem: Which command prints the full path of the directory you are currently working in?
questions.explanation: `pwd` stands for print working directory and writes the absolute path of where you are right now, for example `/home/ama`. `cd` changes directory, `ls` lists contents, and `whereami` is not a standard command.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the command `ls` do by default?
options: Deletes the files in the current directory
Lists every user logged into the system
Lists the names of the files and directories in the current directory
questions.explanation: `ls` (list) prints the names of the files and subdirectories contained in the current directory. By itself it does not delete anything or change location; `rm` deletes and `cd` changes directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command would you use to create a new, empty directory named `data`?
questions.explanation: `mkdir data` (make directory) creates a new empty directory called `data`. `touch data` would instead create an empty file, `cd data` tries to move into it, and `rm data` would try to remove it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What is the effect of running `cd ..` ?
options: Moves up one level to the parent directory
Returns you to your home directory
Lists the parent directory without moving
questions.explanation: The name `..` always refers to the parent directory, so `cd ..` moves you one level up the file-system tree. A single dot `.` means the current directory, and `cd` with no argument is what returns you to your home directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which of the following is an absolute path?
questions.explanation: An absolute path starts from the root of the file-system tree and therefore begins with a leading slash `/`, as in `/home/ama/reports/jan.txt`. The other three are relative paths, interpreted starting from the current directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the command `cp notes.txt backup.txt` do?
options: Makes a copy of `notes.txt` called `backup.txt`, leaving the original in place
questions.explanation: `cp` (copy) duplicates the first file to the second name, so after `cp notes.txt backup.txt` both `notes.txt` and `backup.txt` exist with the same contents. Renaming or moving without keeping the original is the job of `mv`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command both renames a file and can move it to another directory?
questions.explanation: `mv` (move) is used both to rename a file (`mv old.txt new.txt`) and to relocate it into another directory (`mv old.txt archive/`). `cp` copies rather than moves, `rm` removes, and `cat` prints contents.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the command `rm report.txt` do?
questions.explanation: `rm` (remove) deletes the named file, so `rm report.txt` removes `report.txt`. There is no recycle bin by default, so the file is gone. Renaming is done with `mv` and copying with `cp`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: You want to print the entire contents of a short file `data.txt` to the screen at once. Which command is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: `cat data.txt` (concatenate) writes the whole file to the screen in one go, which is ideal for short files. `touch` would create or update a timestamp, `mkdir` makes a directory, and `cd` tries to change into it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command lets you view a long file one screen at a time, scrolling forwards and backwards?
questions.explanation: `less` is a pager: it shows a long file one screenful at a time and lets you scroll up and down and search. `cat` would dump the whole file at once, which scrolls a long file off the screen.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `man` command, as in `man ls` ?
options: It displays the manual (help) page describing how to use `ls`
It lists all manuals you have written
questions.explanation: `man` shows the manual page for a command, so `man ls` displays documentation explaining what `ls` does and its options. It is the standard way to get help on the command line.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `touch results.txt` do when `results.txt` does not yet exist?
options: Creates a new empty file called `results.txt`
questions.explanation: If the file does not exist, `touch results.txt` creates a new empty file with that name. If it already exists, `touch` simply updates its last-modified timestamp without changing the contents.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: At the top of the Linux file-system tree, every path ultimately starts from which directory?
questions.explanation: The whole Linux file system is a single tree whose top is the root directory, written as a single slash `/`. Every absolute path is described starting from `/`. The home directory `~` and `/bin` are just branches below the root.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: In a path, what does a single dot `.` refer to?
questions.explanation: A single dot `.` means the current directory, which is why you run a local script with `./script.sh`. Two dots `..` mean the parent directory, `/` is the root, and `~` is your home directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command shows a long listing with extra details such as permissions, owner and size?
questions.explanation: `ls -l` gives the long listing format, with one entry per line showing permissions, link count, owner, group, size and modification time. `ls -a` shows hidden files and `ls -R` recurses into subdirectories.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which option makes `ls` also show hidden files (those whose names begin with a dot)?
questions.explanation: `ls -a` (all) includes entries that start with a dot, such as `.bashrc`, which are normally hidden. `ls -l` controls the long format, not whether hidden files appear.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: You are in `/home/kofi`. After running `cd projects`, what is your working directory?
questions.explanation: Because `projects` is a relative path, it is appended to where you already are. Starting in `/home/kofi`, `cd projects` moves you to `/home/kofi/projects`. A leading slash would have made it absolute.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command would remove an empty directory called `temp`?
questions.explanation: `rmdir temp` removes a directory, but only if it is empty. Plain `rm temp` refuses to delete a directory without the recursive option `-r`, and `mkdir` does the opposite by creating one.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the shorthand `~` stand for on the command line?
questions.explanation: The tilde `~` is shorthand for your home directory, so `cd ~` or simply `cd` takes you home, and `~/notes.txt` refers to `notes.txt` in your home directory. The root directory is `/` and the current directory is `.`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: How does a relative path differ from an absolute path?
options: A relative path is interpreted starting from the current directory, while an absolute path starts from the root `/`
A relative path must always be longer than an absolute path
A relative path can only point to files, never directories
There is no difference; the two terms mean the same thing
questions.explanation: An absolute path is given in full starting from the root directory `/`, so it is the same no matter where you are. A relative path is interpreted starting from your current working directory, so its meaning depends on where you happen to be.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command copies a whole directory `src` and all of its contents to a new directory `backup`?
questions.explanation: Copying a directory together with everything inside it requires the recursive option, so `cp -r src backup` is correct. Plain `cp src backup` refuses to copy a directory, and `mv` would move rather than copy it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: You run `cat a.txt b.txt`. What is printed?
options: The contents of `a.txt` followed by the contents of `b.txt`
An error, because `cat` takes only one file
questions.explanation: `cat` concatenates its arguments, so `cat a.txt b.txt` prints the full contents of `a.txt` immediately followed by the full contents of `b.txt`. It happily accepts several files at once.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which single command creates the directory `a` and its subdirectory `b` in one step, even though `a` does not exist yet?
questions.explanation: The `-p` (parents) option tells `mkdir` to create any missing parent directories, so `mkdir -p a/b` makes `a` and then `b` inside it. Plain `mkdir a/b` fails because the parent `a` does not yet exist.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the command `clear` do at the terminal?
options: Deletes all files in the current directory
Clears the terminal screen, giving you a blank prompt
Logs you out of the system
questions.explanation: `clear` simply wipes the visible terminal screen and places the prompt at the top; it does not delete files, log you out, or erase your saved history. It is purely cosmetic, to tidy a cluttered screen.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Fill in the directory-navigation commands.
questions.solution: To print the absolute path of where you currently are, you run {{1}}. To move up to the parent directory, you run {{2}}. To return to your home directory with no argument, you run {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `pwd` prints the working directory, `cd ..` moves to the parent directory using the `..` shorthand, and `cd` with no argument (equivalently `cd ~`) returns you to your home directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the command for each file operation.
questions.solution: To create a new empty file named `log.txt`, use {{1}}. To make a copy of it called `log.bak`, use {{2}}. To delete the original file, use {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `touch` creates an empty file, `cp source target` copies a file to a new name while keeping the original, and `rm` removes the named file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: You start in the directory `/home/efua`. Reason about where each command leaves you.
questions.solution: After running `cd reports`, your working directory has 4 components counting from the root; the number of path components is {{1}}. The shorthand for that same home directory `/home/efua` is the symbol picked here: {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Starting in `/home/efua` and running the relative `cd reports` gives `/home/efua/reports`, whose four named components are `home`, `efua` and `reports` below the root, so counting the root level there are 4 components in `/home/efua/reports`. The tilde `~` is the shorthand for your home directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Match each task to the right listing or help command.
questions.solution: To see a long listing with permissions and sizes, run {{1}}. To include hidden dot-files in the listing, run {{2}}. To read the manual page for the `cp` command, run {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `ls -l` gives the long listing with permissions and sizes, `ls -a` adds hidden dot-files, and `man cp` opens the manual page that documents `cp`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the right command for each directory task.
questions.solution: To create a new empty directory called `data`, run {{1}}. To copy the whole directory `src` and everything inside it to `backup`, run {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `mkdir data` makes a new empty directory, and copying a directory with its contents needs the recursive option, so `cp -r src backup` is correct while plain `cp` refuses to copy a directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Identify the path types in `/home/yaa/data.txt`.
questions.solution: The path `/home/yaa/data.txt` is described here as {{1}} because it begins at the root. The character that always begins such a path is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: A path that starts from the root directory is an absolute path, and it always begins with a leading slash `/`. A relative path would instead start from the current directory.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)
